Until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the position was traditionally held by members of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}, particularly from two specific lineages. == Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮}}, but it was never realized.

The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|12=draft}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|12=draft}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|12=draft}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=draft}}).</small> == See Also ==
* {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|12=draft}} * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=draft}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|12=draft}} (Imperial princess priestess)
== References ==
<!--Saishu (Ise Shrine)-->
{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto priesthood]] [[Category:Historical government positions in Japan]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Saishu (Ise Shrine)]] [[en:Saishu (Ise Shrine)]] [[ja:祭主]] [[sn:aishu (Ise Shrine)]]

The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|12=draft}}, exclusively found at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is {{ill|Sayako Kuroda|ja|黒田清子|12=draft}}.
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=draft}}, under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|12=draft}}, serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=draft}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|12=draft}} when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=draft}} (father of {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|ja|藤原鎌足|12=draft}}) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=draft}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, the position was traditionally held by members of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|12=draft}}, particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=draft}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Jingi Gon Taifu|ja|神祇権大副|12=draft}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}} during major festivals like the {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}} (June and December), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}, offering {{ill|norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft}} (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=draft}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|12=draft}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=draft}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=draft}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|12=draft}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|ja|神道指令|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|ja|日本国憲法|12=draft}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=draft}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=draft}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|12=draft}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|12=draft}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|12=draft}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=draft}}).</small> == See Also ==
* {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|12=draft}} * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=draft}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|12=draft}} (Imperial princess priestess)

The position originated during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (father of {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|ja|藤原鎌足|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the position was traditionally held by members of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Jingi Gon Taifu|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during major festivals like the {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June and December), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, offering {{ill|norito|ja|祝詞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|ja|神道指令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|ja|日本国憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).</small> == See Also ==
* {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Imperial princess priestess)

It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is {{ill|Sayako Kuroda|ja|黒田清子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. During the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|ja|神道指令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|ja|日本国憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).</small> == See Also ==
* {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Imperial princess priestess)

<!--Saishu (Ise Shrine)-->

The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, exclusively found at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is {{ill|Sayako Kuroda|ja|黒田清子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (father of {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|ja|藤原鎌足|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the position was traditionally held by members of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Jingi Gon Taifu|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during major festivals like the {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June and December), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, offering {{ill|norito|ja|祝詞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|ja|神道指令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|ja|日本国憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).</small> == See Also ==
* {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Imperial princess priestess)

Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiō|qq=}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=}}).</small> == See Also ==
* {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiō|qq=}} (Imperial princess priestess)

<!--Saishu (Ise Shrine)-->

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Jingi Gon Taifu]] → [[Department of Divinities]]
revision.text:
The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}}, exclusively found at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is {{ill|Sayako Kuroda|ja|黒田清子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sayako Kuroda|qq=}}.
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi-kan|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}}, serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko|qq=}} when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=}} (father of {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|ja|藤原鎌足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Kamatari|qq=}}) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the position was traditionally held by members of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=}}, particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}} during major festivals like the {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinensai|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niinamesai|qq=}}, offering {{ill|norito|ja|祝詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/norito|qq=}} (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kazoku|qq=}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|ja|神道指令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Directive|qq=}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|ja|日本国憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Constitution of Japan|qq=}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Asahiko of Kuni|qq=}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|qq=}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiō|qq=}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=}}).</small> == See Also ==
* {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiō|qq=}} (Imperial princess priestess)
== References ==

The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kazoku|qq=}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|ja|神道指令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Directive|qq=}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|ja|日本国憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Constitution of Japan|qq=}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Asahiko of Kuni|qq=}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|qq=}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiō|qq=}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.

[[sn:Saishu (Ise Shrine)]] [[en:Saishu (Ise Shrine)]] [[ja:祭主]] [[sn:aishu (Ise Shrine)]]
<!--Saishu (Ise Shrine)-->

The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}}, exclusively found at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is {{ill|Sayako Kuroda|ja|黒田清子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sayako Kuroda|qq=}}.
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi-kan|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}}, serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko|qq=}} when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=}} (father of {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|ja|藤原鎌足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Kamatari|qq=}}) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the position was traditionally held by members of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=}}, particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}} during major festivals like the {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinensai|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niinamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niinamesai|qq=}}, offering {{ill|norito|ja|祝詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/norito|qq=}} (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kazoku|qq=}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|ja|神道指令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Directive|qq=}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|ja|日本国憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Constitution of Japan|qq=}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Asahiko of Kuni|qq=}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|qq=}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiō|qq=}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.

The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}} during major festivals like the {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinensai|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niinamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niinamesai|qq=}}, offering {{ill|norito|ja|祝詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/norito|qq=}} (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kazoku|qq=}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|ja|神道指令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Directive|qq=}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|ja|日本国憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Constitution of Japan|qq=}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Asahiko of Kuni|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Asahiko of Kuni|qq=}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|ja|熱田神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|qq=}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiō|qq=}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==

* {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神職|Kannushi=Kannushi|lt=Shinto priesthood|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saiō|ja|斎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saiō|qq=}} (Imperial princess priestess)

revision.text:
The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryōge no Kan|lt=Ryōge no Kan|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=}}).</small> == See Also ==

The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryōge no Kan|lt=Ryōge no Kan|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)
== References ==

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|神宮式年遷宮|" to "|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|"
revision.text:
The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryōge no Kan|lt=Ryōge no Kan|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)
== References ==

Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Shinto priesthood
revision.text:
The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|Ryōge no Kan|lt=Ryōge no Kan|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)

The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=Metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==

Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)

<!--Saishu (Ise Shrine)--> [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Abolished Administrative Positions in Japan]] [[Category:Chief Festival Officiants]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Saishu (Ise Shrine)]][[Category:qq]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)
