<!--Saishu (Ise Shrine)--> [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Abolished Administrative Positions in Japan]] [[Category:Chief Festival Officiants]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[en:Saishu (Ise Shrine)]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"Kanshoku Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)

As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|qq=|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)

It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>

During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.

During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|qq=|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|qq=|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==

During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.

Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.

<!--Saishu (Ise Shrine)--> [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Abolished Administrative Positions in Japan]] [[Category:Chief Festival Officiants]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11591085}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==

In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)
== References ==

During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.

After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is [[:en:Sayako Kuroda|Sayako Kuroda]].
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the position was traditionally held by members of the [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]], particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] during major festivals like the [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Niinamesai]], offering [[:en:Norito|norito]] (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and [[:en:Kazoku|kazoku]] (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], under the [[:en:Shinto Directive|Shinto Directive]] and the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]], the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Asahiko of Kuni]], and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * [[:en:Saikū|Saiō]] (Imperial princess priestess)
== References ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591085]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is {{ill|Sayako Kuroda|en|Sayako Kuroda|WD=Q229333|lt=Sayako Kuroda}}.
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}, serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Fujiwara no Kamatari}}) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, the position was traditionally held by members of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|WD=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan}}, particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Jingi-kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}} during major festivals like the {{ill|Kinen-sai|en|Kinen-sai|WD=Q118906710|lt=Kinensai}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|en|Kannamesai Festival|WD=Q11255045|lt=Niinamesai}}, offering {{ill|Norito|en|Norito|WD=Q1760119|lt=norito}} (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|Kazoku|en|Kazoku|WD=Q241734|lt=kazoku}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Kuni Asahiko|en|Prince Kuni Asahiko|WD=Q5370435|lt=Prince Asahiko of Kuni}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|Atsuta Shrine|WD=Q482065|lt=Atsuta Shrine}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saiō}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saiō}} (Imperial princess priestess)
== References ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[eo:Saishu]][[ja:祭主]]
The '''Saishu''' (祭主) is a position within the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]], exclusively found at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. It can also be read as "Matsuri no Tsukasa." The current Saishu is {{ill|Sayako Kuroda|en|Sayako Kuroda|WD=Q229333|lt=Sayako Kuroda|1=Sayako Kuroda}}.
In ancient times, the Saishu was one of the {{ill|East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=East Asian extra-statutory office|lt=Ryougai-kan|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryougai-kan|qq=}}, under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}}, serving as the chief priest of Ise Grand Shrine. As a central government official, the Saishu usually resided in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124313007}}, engaging in administrative affairs related to the shrine.
The position originated during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} when {{ill|Nakatomi no Mikeko|ja|中臣御食子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Mikeko|1=Nakatomi no Mikeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366911}} (father of {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|WD=Q433995|lt=Fujiwara no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}}) was appointed as the first '''Saikan''' (祭官). During the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}, the title was changed to '''Saishu''' when {{ill|Nakatomi no Omi-maro|ja|中臣意美麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Omi-maro|1=Nakatomi no Omimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366913}} assumed the role.<ref>"Nisho Daijingū Reibun," Section 8, Saishu Succession: "The title was changed from Saikan to Saishu when Omi-maro took office."</ref>
Until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the position was traditionally held by members of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|WD=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}}, particularly from two specific lineages. It was often concurrently held with the roles of {{ill|Jingi Taifu|ja|神祇大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2264720}} (Vice Minister of Divinities) or {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇権大副|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/|lt=Jingi Gon Taifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神祇官}}.<ref>"East Asian government position Hisyo," Jingi-kan, Saishu Section: "The Saishu was typically chosen from the second branch of the Nakatomi clan."</ref>
The Saishu’s primary role is to represent the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} during major festivals like the {{ill|Kinen-sai|en|Kinen-sai|WD=Q118906710|lt=Kinensai|1=Kinensai}}, {{ill|Tsukinamisai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinamisai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (June and December), and {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|en|Kannamesai Festival|WD=Q11255045|lt=Niinamesai|1=Kannamesai Festival}}, offering {{ill|Norito|en|Norito|WD=Q1760119|lt=norito|1=Norito}} (prayers) to convey the Emperor's will to the enshrined deities. During the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}} (periodic shrine rebuilding), the Saishu also serves as the '''Zojingushi''' (造神宮使) or '''Hosen-shi''' (奉遷使).
Although similar titles existed in other shrines, the Saishu of Ise Grand Shrine remains distinct.
== Modern Period ==
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the hereditary system of shrine priesthood was abolished. The monopoly of the {{ill|Fujiwara family|ja|藤原家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原氏|1=Fujiwara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q641628}} over the Saishu role ended, and {{ill|Kazoku|en|Kazoku|WD=Q241734|lt=kazoku|1=Kazoku}} (nobles) were appointed instead. Later, it became a {{ill|Shinninkan|lt=Shinninkan|lt=Chokuninkan|ja|親任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokuninkan|qq=}} (imperial appointment), formalized under the {{ill|Jingu Shicho|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Shicho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q687168}} system by imperial decree.
Following {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive|1=Shinto Directive}} and the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}}, the Ise Grand Shrine became an independent {{ill|religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/religious corporation|qq=}}. Under its internal regulations, the Saishu is now appointed based on an imperial mandate. Since then, the role has predominantly been held by {{ill|female former members of the imperial family|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/female former members of the imperial family|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}.
The first imperial Saishu of the modern era was {{ill|Prince Kuni Asahiko|en|Prince Kuni Asahiko|WD=Q5370435|lt=Prince Asahiko of Kuni|1=Prince Kuni Asahiko}}, and his descendants frequently assumed the role. There was a movement to establish a Saishu position at {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|Atsuta Shrine|WD=Q482065|lt=Atsuta Shrine|1=Atsuta Shrine}}, but it was never realized.
Although often confused with the historical role of {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saiō|1=Saikū}} (imperial princess serving as high priestess at Ise), the Saishu is distinct. The Saiō tradition ended during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, while the modern Saishu continues today.
== References ==
<small>Refer to historical records and academic works, including Nitta Hitoshi's "Ise Grand Shrine in the Meiji Era" ({{ill|Kogakkan University Journal|ja|皇学館論叢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogakkan University Journal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).</small> == See Also ==
* [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priesthood]] * {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453500}} (Chief priest) * {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|WD=Q150845|lt=Saiō|1=Saikū}} (Imperial princess priestess)
== References ==
